risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]." Comment: A computerized urn randomization procedure is an explicitly random allocation method, and the paper also notes the groups were balanced on several baseline factors, supporting truly random sequence generation.

Quote: "Those who agreed to participate were randomized to either the Standard Treatment group (STD; n=49) or to the Standard Treatment + Cognitive-Behavioral Treatment group (STD+CBT; n=52) using a computerized urn randomization procedure [28]. ... The Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session and informed the participants of their treatment assignments." Comment: The study explicitly states that a computerized urn randomization procedure was used and that the Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session. While urn randomization is not one of the listed mechanisms, this description indicates allocation was handled centrally by a coordinator rather than by the recruiter/enroller, which would prevent foreknowledge of the next assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The Project Coordinator entered the urn data during the intake session and informed the participants of their treatment assignments." and "Patients were told that the treatment was intended to change oral habits..." / "In addition, patients received a brief cognitive-behavioral program..." Comment: Participants were explicitly informed of allocation, and the two arms involved clearly different active treatments (standard splint/medication/soft diet versus the same plus CBT), so blinding of participants and treating personnel was not feasible. Because the main outcomes were self-reported pain, depression, and interference, knowledge of assignment could plausibly influence outcomes, indicating high performance bias risk.

Quote: "A trained M.A.-level research associate, who was not blinded to treatment condition, conducted the pretreatment and follow-up research assessments." Comment: The outcome assessor was explicitly unblinded. The primary outcomes were patient-reported pain, depressive symptoms, and pain-related interference (e.g., MPI and CES-D), which are subjective and could be influenced by knowledge of allocation during interview-based assessment, so detection bias is likely.

Quote: "At posttreatment 88% of patients provided data, and 73% provided data at 52 weeks. Losses to follow-up were equivalent across treatment conditions." Comment: Attrition was moderate but not excessive, and the paper explicitly states it was balanced across groups. The primary analyses used a mixed model regression procedure with an intent-to-treat approach, which appropriately uses all available data. Overall, the missing data do not appear likely to bias the result.
